population: Highly trained and elite middle- and long-distance runners across 10 articles
intervention: Periodization methods, volume, and training-intensity distribution
comparator: No direct seven-day versus nonweekly comparator
component_dose: NOT_VERIFIED
outcome_measure: Periodization and intensity-distribution patterns
effect_estimate: Hard/easy practice described; no cycle-length effect estimate
uncertainty: Abstract only; 10 included articles
limitations: No exact 9-, 9.5-, or 10-day comparison; no adolescent-specific conclusion

population: Middle- and long-distance runners across 20 articles
intervention: Training-intensity distribution classified by multiple methods
component_dose: NOT_VERIFIED
outcome_measure: Intensity-distribution classification
instrument_protocol: sRPE, heart rate, lactate, race pace, and running speed methods
effect_estimate: Classification differed materially by method; no frame effect
uncertainty: Abstract only; heterogeneous measurement methods
limitations: Cannot select a 9.5-day duration or collapse unlike intensity measures

population: 348 trained endurance athletes in 13 studies
intervention: Training-intensity distribution interventions
comparator: Polarized versus pyramidal and other distributions
component_dose: NOT_VERIFIED
outcome_measure: VO2max change
effect_estimate: No statistically significant polarized-versus-pyramidal difference reported in abstract
uncertainty: Mixed sports and few female athletes; abstract only
individual_response: Individual-participant analysis used but response details not verified
limitations: Not a calendar-frame comparison and cannot determine 9.5-day mechanics

population: Trained and well-trained adult-dominant endurance athletes across 20 eligible records
intervention: Block periodization
component_dose: Concentrated blocks varied across studies
outcome_measure: VO2max and maximal aerobic power
effect_estimate: Small favorable VO2max and Wmax effects reported
uncertainty: Small, heterogeneous, low-quality evidence
limitations: No seven-day versus nonweekly or 9.5-day comparison

population: Humans represented in periodization meta-analyses; underlying studies were strength training
intervention: Periodized exercise programs
comparator: Non-periodized exercise programs as defined in prior meta-analyses
component_dose: NOT_VERIFIED
outcome_measure: Comparator validity and adaptation-timing assumptions
effect_estimate: Audited literature did not test varied nonperiodized comparators or timing-prediction accuracy
uncertainty: Indirect to running and dependent on underlying review quality
limitations: Cannot justify a running microcycle duration or 9.5-day optimality

population: 106 coaches; 58% individual-sport coaches and 32% coaching international-level athletes
intervention: Survey of common planning concepts
component_dose: NOT_APPLICABLE
outcome_measure: Use and perceived usefulness of planning concepts
effect_estimate: Coach perceptions support revisable planning indirectly
uncertainty: No runner subgroup, cycle prevalence, or outcome efficacy
limitations: Perception evidence cannot establish 9.5-day efficacy or safety

population: 12 successful male Norwegian coaches across eight Olympic endurance sports
event: Long-distance running and seven other Olympic endurance sports
intervention: Best-practice training characteristics
component_dose: NOT_VERIFIED
component_order: Weekly organization remained explicit with pragmatic adjustments
outcome_measure: Weekly structure and contextual adjustment
effect_estimate: Weekly organization and flexible adjustment were both reported
uncertainty: Middle-distance events under six minutes excluded; descriptive design
limitations: Counterevidence to claiming elite endurance practice abandons weeks; no efficacy inference

population: World-class adult distance runners
intervention: Typical training characteristics
component_dose: 11-14 sessions per week
outcome_measure: Sessions per week and typical training weeks
instrument_protocol: Narrative integration of literature and results-proven practice
effect_estimate: Reports 11-14 sessions per week
uncertainty: Narrative evidence and no frame comparison
limitations: Adult long-distance practice only; no 9.5-day efficacy or youth safety inference

population: Eight elite adult middle-distance runners
intervention: Live-high train-high camp periodization
component_dose: Individual training quantified during camp
component_order: Typical training week with event and altitude adjustments
outcome_measure: Training quantification and individual adjustment
effect_estimate: Weekly structure coexisted with individual event and altitude adjustments
uncertainty: n=8; altitude context; observational; no comparator
individual_response: Individual adjustments described but not causally evaluated
limitations: Does not authorize 9.5-day efficacy, safety, or a fixed MAIN count

population: 13 healthy adult runners
competitive_level: At least 20 miles per week and VO2 criteria
intervention: Approximately 9-9.5 consecutive days of twice-daily supervised intervals plus glutamine or placebo
component_dose: Twice-daily intervals; exact session dose not used for frame inference
outcome_measure: Nasal and salivary IgA; glutamine; compliance
time_horizon: Short overload and five recovery days
effect_estimate: Primary mucosal-marker findings; compliance 96%
uncertainty: n=13; supplement question; not a recurring microcycle; no frame comparator
limitations: Exact-duration false positive; prohibited as evidence for 9.5-day Formation

population: Meb Keflezighi, elite adult male marathoner
intervention: Nine-day training cycle
component_dose: One tempo, one interval, and one long run per nine-day cycle; other running by feel
outcome_measure: Named cycle use; Boston win and 2:08:37 personal best context
effect_estimate: Verified self-report of actual nine-day use; no causal effect estimate
uncertainty: Single retrospective case; adult marathon; injury-history context
limitations: Performance cannot be attributed to the cycle; no youth, safety, or 9.5-day validation

population: Michael Ko, adult recreational male marathoner
intervention: Nine-day self-programmed cycle
component_dose: Threshold/easy/easy repeated; third quality exposure was a long run
component_order: Three quality exposures with two easy days between
outcome_measure: Named cycle use; personal best; subjective recovery and enjoyment
effect_estimate: Self-reported 2:56 personal best and better recovery/enjoyment
uncertainty: Single case; concurrent changes; weekday feasibility concerns
limitations: Cannot establish causal benefit, recovery completion, youth suitability, or 9.5-day superiority

population: Adult distance runners and coaches including Lindsey Scherf and Thom Hunt
intervention: Nine-, ten-, and fourteen-day templates and reported practice
component_dose: Templates include quality, long-run, and recovery days
separation_hours: A quoted 72 h preference appears in source
outcome_measure: Actual ten-day use; schedule and social collisions
instrument_protocol: Coach and athlete interviews plus practice article
effect_estimate: Thom Hunt reports actual ten-day use; nine-day template only
uncertainty: Anecdotal source; multiple templates; no outcome testing
limitations: The 72 h statement is preference only, never clearance; no 9.5-day efficacy or safety inference

population: Distance runners; named Hansons program and Ray Treacy athletes
competitive_level: Elite program athletes and runners broadly
intervention: Reported ten-day to two-week cycles
component_dose: NOT_VERIFIED
outcome_measure: Reported program use and scheduling rationale
effect_estimate: Third-party report of ten-day use; no measured effect
uncertainty: No primary opened protocol, denominator, or cohort outcomes
limitations: Unsupported injury and benefit language excluded; no prevalence, efficacy, or safety evidence

population: Runners broadly
intervention: Coach-authored nine-day sample plan with ten-day mention
component_dose: Long run, two quality sessions, easy/rest, strength/cross-training, and recovery
outcome_measure: Verifiable sample-plan structure
effect_estimate: No measured outcome; template existence only
uncertainty: No named implemented cohort and no outcome evaluation
limitations: Can support intelligibility only; not common use, optimality, safety, or numerical policy

population: Sprint and hurdle athletes at high-school, college, and professional levels
intervention: Ten-day in-season setup
component_dose: Speed, tempo, regeneration, strength, off, and flex days
outcome_measure: Verifiable ten-day setup and meet-schedule rationale
effect_estimate: No measured outcome or verified actual use
uncertainty: Wrong event directness; coach presentation; no sample
limitations: Supports competition-anchor concept only; cannot determine youth middle-distance dose or 9.5-day safety

population: Emma Bates, elite adult female marathoner
intervention: Ten-day training cycle
component_dose: Two workouts and one long run followed by three recovery days after long run
outcome_measure: Verified ten-day use; Boston finish context
effect_estimate: Fifth place in 2:22:10 reported; no causal effect estimate
uncertainty: Single athlete; adult marathon; no comparator
limitations: Corrects erroneous nine-day secondary report; performance cannot validate 9.5 days

population: 12 trained adolescent male runners
intervention: All-out 21-km run
component_dose: 21 km all-out
outcome_measure: TBARS, uric acid, glutathione, XO, SOD, CAT
effect_estimate: CAT and TBARS remained elevated at 24 h versus pre-run
uncertainty: n=12; male only; abstract only; no performance or neuromuscular outcomes
limitations: Extreme endurance bout and biomarker-only evidence; cannot diagnose, clear training, or set spacing

population: 10 trained adolescent male runners
intervention: All-out 21-km run
component_dose: 21 km all-out
outcome_measure: Urea, creatinine, CK, LDH, hemoglobin, hematocrit, eGFR, plasma-volume change
instrument_protocol: Blood sampling and calculated renal markers; abstract-level protocol
effect_estimate: Early biochemical changes were sustained at 24 h
uncertainty: n=10; male only; abstract only; hydration-sensitive calculations
limitations: No performance, force, or gait measure; biomarkers cannot determine safety or next-MAIN clearance

population: 12 trained male adolescent multi-sport endurance athletes
intervention: Trail running, cycling, kayaking, and skating races ten weeks apart
comparator: Pre-race baseline and within-athlete alternate race format
component_dose: 48.2 km one-day; 66.0 km two-day
outcome_measure: Drop jump, squat jump, MVIC, soreness, cTnI, CK-MB
instrument_protocol: Jump tests, isometric force, soreness scale, blood biomarkers
time_points: Pre; post; 24-72 h depending on marker
missingness: No blood draw after day 1 of the two-day race
effect_estimate: Drop-jump height -5.9% post; MVIC unchanged; soreness not different at 72 h; most cardiac markers toward baseline by 24-48 h
uncertainty: n=12; male only; mixed modalities and durations; marker-specific follow-up
limitations: Young-athlete but not middle-distance evidence; marker trajectories cannot establish whole-athlete recovery or safety

population: 14 elite youth soccer players
intervention: Official match with no subsequent training
component_dose: 90-minute match
outcome_measure: Isometric posterior-chain peak force
instrument_protocol: Posterior-chain isometric test at multiple joint angles
effect_estimate: Peak force -18% post, -8% at 24 h, not different at 48 h, and +12% at 72 h
uncertainty: One team and one match; repeated familiarization may explain 72-h value
individual_response: Large individual and joint-angle variation reported
limitations: Single force test and indirect soccer population cannot clear a runner or define 72 h

population: 10 male elite youth academy soccer players
intervention: Post-match static stretching or passive recovery in crossover
component_dose: Competitive match plus assigned recovery condition
outcome_measure: CMJ, soreness, CK
instrument_protocol: Countermovement jump, soreness scale, creatine kinase
effect_estimate: CMJ remained lower and soreness/CK remained elevated at 48 h in both conditions
uncertainty: n=10; intervention confounds natural kinetics; soccer-specific
limitations: Cannot set a runner interval or treat stretching as restoration

population: 42 male elite youth soccer players
intervention: Official match
component_dose: NOT_VERIFIED
outcome_measure: Low-frequency fatigue, CMJ, subjective fatigue, soreness, recovery perception
effect_estimate: Low-frequency fatigue not different at 24 h and CMJ unchanged; subjective and soreness measures remained impaired through 48 h
uncertainty: Abstract only; male soccer; incomplete outcome coverage
individual_response: Outcome domains disagreed within the same study
limitations: Evidence requires domain separation and cannot be normalized to recovered

population: 48 Italian Serie A youth soccer players
event: Friendly soccer match plus training at 48 h
intervention: Match followed by complete or reduced training at 48 h
comparator: Complete training n=26 versus reduced training n=22
component_dose: Intervening training load at 48 h
outcome_measure: Posterior-chain force, CMJ, soreness, subjective fatigue
instrument_protocol: Force and jump tests plus self-report
effect_estimate: At 48 h force and soreness remained altered while CMJ and fatigue did not; complete training reduced CMJ/force from 48 to 72 h versus reduced training
uncertainty: Friendly match; no biochemical outcomes; 72-h value includes intervening session
limitations: Shows elapsed time cannot identify causal session or current state; indirect to running

population: 73 U19 and U23 soccer players with participation and non-participation observations
intervention: Match participation over 60 min versus non-participation; training loads recorded
component_dose: Match and intervening training load
outcome_measure: IMTP peak force
instrument_protocol: Isometric mid-thigh pull plus recorded match/training loads
effect_estimate: Force -8.1% at 48 h and -6.2% at 72 h in participants; non-participants -8.2% at 72 h; load metrics not correlated with change
uncertainty: Mixed U19/U23; one force test; superimposed training prevents match-only attribution
limitations: Counterevidence to clearance but not proof of a universal interval or match-only causality

population: 32 male U17/U19 elite academy soccer players with limited structured-strength experience
intervention: Back squats plus calf raises versus back squats plus hurdle/drop jumps in crossover
component_dose: Shared squat stimulus; exact sets and repetitions verified in full text but not normalized here
outcome_measure: CMJ, drop jump, IMTP, adductor strength, self-report, CK
instrument_protocol: Field tests, strength tests, self-report, creatine kinase
missingness: No sprint or central-fatigue test; sleep/nutrition uncontrolled
effect_estimate: Largest fatigue at 24-48 h; CK peaked at 48 h; selected markers near baseline by 72 h; no modality-by-time interaction
uncertainty: Non-random order; unfamiliar strength work; shared squat stimulus
limitations: Selected-marker behavior is not safety, clearance, or whole-athlete recovery

population: Youth and adult participants in systematic review/meta-analysis
intervention: Youth-adult comparisons without recovery treatment
component_dose: Sessions varied across included studies
outcome_measure: Peak strength loss, soreness, CK
missingness: Session-specific kinetics not available in abstract
effect_estimate: Peak force, soreness, and CK effects were larger in adults in pooled comparisons
uncertainty: Abstract only; no runner stratum or time-to-normalization estimate
limitations: Peak magnitude is not a universal interval and cannot clear an individual adolescent

population: 12 national-standard adult 1500-m specialists; 10 male and 2 female
intervention: Two 1500-m time trials 24 h apart
component_dose: Self-paced 1500 m
outcome_measure: Time, speed, step variables, physiological measures, exertion
instrument_protocol: Treadmill 1500-m trial, force-platform and physiological testing
effect_estimate: Performance and most measures differed no more than prior measurement error; exertion about 2.5 times more likely higher on trial 2
uncertainty: n=12; adult treadmill trial; early season; carbon shoes; no tactical race
individual_response: Considerable uncertainty and within-athlete exertion variation
limitations: Maintained one outcome at 24 h does not establish complete recovery or safe MAIN repetition

population: 20 recreational masters club runners; 10 female and 10 male
intervention: Six 800-m HIIT repetitions
component_dose: 6 x 800 m; recovery dose NOT_VERIFIED
outcome_measure: Hip/knee kinematics, coordination, maximal isometric force, voluntary activation, twitch
instrument_protocol: Six-minute medium run for gait plus force and neuromuscular tests
effect_estimate: Several group deficits at 24 h by NHST; MDC classified most but not all individuals within measurement variability
uncertainty: n=20; masters; conclusion depends on statistical method
individual_response: MDC analysis showed heterogeneous individual classifications
limitations: Runner-direct but adult; cannot establish complete recovery or injury risk

population: 26 adult recreational male runners
intervention: 60-min downhill run at -10% and 65% maximal HR
component_dose: 60 min downhill running
outcome_measure: Vertical ground-reaction-force peaks, CK, MRI T2, pain
instrument_protocol: Force-platform gait, blood CK, MRI; abstract-level extraction
missingness: Pain result not reported in abstract; no 72-h point
effect_estimate: Selected force peaks reduced through 24-48 h; CK elevated at 24 and 48 h; anterior-thigh MRI T2 elevated to 48 h
uncertainty: Abstract only; male recreational sample; contrived eccentric-damage session
limitations: Asymmetric and session-specific findings cannot set a universal runner interval

population: 10 recreational male runners; CK subset n=6
event: Two downhill-running bouts three weeks apart
intervention: Two 30-min downhill bouts at -20% and 2.8 m/s
component_dose: 2 x 30 min downhill exposures separated three weeks
outcome_measure: MVC, peripheral indices, gait, soreness, CK
instrument_protocol: Treadmill downhill running with force, neuromuscular and blood measures
time_points: Pre; post; 24 h; 48 h; 72 h; 96 h; 168 h
missingness: CK timing internally inconsistent between methods and results
effect_estimate: Bout 1 MVC lower through 48 h, gait altered to 72 h, soreness elevated through 96 h and not different at 168 h; bout 2 smaller/shorter
uncertainty: n=10 men; CK n=6; fixed speed; strong repeated-bout effect
limitations: Explicit residual beyond 72 h but no universal replacement interval or clearance rule

population: 10 competitive sprinters, long jumpers, and soccer players
intervention: Unresisted, 10%-body-mass resisted, 20%-body-mass resisted, and control conditions
component_dose: 2 x 3 x 20 m plus 1 x 3 x 30 m
outcome_measure: 30-m sprint, CMJ, eccentric torque, DOMS, CK
instrument_protocol: Sprint timing, jump, dynamometry, soreness and blood measures
effect_estimate: CK/DOMS elevated to 72 h; 30-m sprint slower through 72 h only after 20% resistance; CMJ unchanged
uncertainty: n=10; mixed sports; crossover/repeated-bout concerns; high CK variability
limitations: Protocol-specific adult evidence; never a universal 72-h or youth-runner rule

population: 12 exercise participants and 12 resting controls; participant training status NOT_VERIFIED
intervention: Intense plyometric exercise
component_dose: Full dose NOT_VERIFIED
outcome_measure: CMJ, squat jump, strength, DOMS, CK, LDH, inflammatory markers
time_points: Pre; post; 24 h; 48 h; 72 h; 96 h; 120 h
missingness: Participant and dose details incomplete in abstract
effect_estimate: CMJ/SJ fell 8-20% and remained impaired as long as 72 h; force unchanged; DOMS/CK/LDH peaked 24-48 h
uncertainty: Abstract only; non-runner; multiple tests; incomplete participant details
limitations: Outcome-specific plyometric residual cannot establish whole-session clearance

population: 9 competitive Ultimate Frisbee players
intervention: Multi-modal plyometric plus resistance session
component_dose: Dose NOT_VERIFIED
outcome_measure: Vertical and broad jump, linear sprint, repeated agility, DOMS, CK
effect_estimate: Jump and linear sprint impaired at 24 and 48 h; agility impaired at 24 h; DOMS and CK elevated at 24 and 48 h
uncertainty: n=9; abstract only; combined components cannot be separated
limitations: Indirect other-sport evidence and no universal interval inference

population: 25 healthy adults
intervention: Cycle ergometer sprint-interval session
component_dose: 8 x 15 s all-out with 2-min rests
outcome_measure: iMVC, evoked force, F0, V0, Pmax, voluntary activation, HRV
instrument_protocol: Cycle ergometer, neuromuscular testing, nocturnal HRV; abstract-level extraction
time_points: Pre; 24 h; 48 h; three subsequent nights for HRV
missingness: No soreness, biochemical, or gait endpoints
effect_estimate: No significant 24/48-h difference in selected measured functions
uncertainty: Abstract only; cycling not running; non-significance is not equivalence
limitations: Title wording cannot be generalized to complete recovery, safety, or clearance

population: 10 adults
intervention: Recumbent-cycle repeated-sprint sessions
component_dose: 10 x 10 s sprints with 30-s rest
outcome_measure: Sprint output, chair MVC, Db100
instrument_protocol: Cycle repeated-sprint and neuromuscular tests; abstract-level extraction
effect_estimate: Sprint output did not differ; at 24 h MVC -6.5% and Db100 -10.4%; deficits not reported at 48 h
uncertainty: n=10; cycling; abstract only; repeated-session design
limitations: Preserved output cannot erase simultaneous neuromuscular change or clear running

population: Healthy adults and adolescents across 20 studies
intervention: Resistance or plyometric muscle-damaging protocols
comparator: Pre-exercise baseline or controls across studies
component_dose: Heterogeneous protocols
outcome_measure: Sprint, change of direction, CMJ, DOMS, CK
time_points: At least 24 h; effects reported through 72 h
missingness: Diet and familiarization controls limited across studies
effect_estimate: Pooled sprint/COD/CMJ impairments and DOMS/CK elevations reported up to 72 h
uncertainty: CK/DOMS heterogeneity I2 65-81%; varied histories and protocols; youth not isolated
limitations: Review overlap and indirectness prevent a universal interval, safety claim, or youth prescription

population: 165 soccer athletes across 11 studies; adult/youth mix and mostly male
intervention: External match load and residual response
comparator: Different load metrics and post-match time points
component_dose: Match exposure across included studies
outcome_measure: High-speed distance, total distance, CK, CMJ peak power
instrument_protocol: Systematic review/meta-analysis of tracking and residual markers
effect_estimate: Distance above 5.5 m/s related to some immediate/24-h markers but not 48/72 h; total distance showed no clear relation
uncertainty: Few studies; soccer-specific; correlational; overlapping primary evidence
limitations: External load alone cannot predict readiness, recovery, or a 72-h spacing rule

population: Team-ball-sport athletes across 28 studies
intervention: Match exposure
comparator: Pre-match baseline and sport/time subgroup comparisons
component_dose: Heterogeneous team-sport matches
outcome_measure: Sprint performance, CK, cortisol and other recovery markers
time_points: At least two post-match measures; up to 144 h
missingness: Primary-study overlap not mapped in this lane
effect_estimate: Performance can return before biochemical markers; CK courses can extend to at least 72 h
uncertainty: Abstract-level extraction; heterogeneous sports and protocols; no direct youth-runner stratum
limitations: Domain disagreement forbids recovered normalization and cannot define a running interval

population: 32 post-pubertal male provincial distance runners
intervention: Complex training versus traditional resistance training alongside running
component_dose: 3 x 60 min/week for 8 weeks; 60-80% 1RM progression; generally 3-4 sets and 9-12 jumps; about 44.5 +/- 6.33 km/week running
component_order: Heavy resistance then biomechanically matched plyometric component
outcome_measure: Economy at 12/14/16 km/h, CMJ power, drop jump, RSI, VO2max, 1RM
instrument_protocol: Running economy, jump, strength and aerobic tests
missingness: Placement of endurance relative to strength on shared days NOT_REPORTED
adverse_events: No adverse events explicitly reported; all 32 completed
effect_estimate: Interactions favored complex training for economy, CMJ power, drop jump and RSI; no interaction for VO2max or 1RM
uncertainty: Short duration; male only; no race-performance outcome; questionable 80% 1RM x 8-12RM notation
limitations: Supports a narrow heavy-to-plyometric pair only; no universal sequence, safety, or 9.5-day inference

population: 25 postpubertal adolescent distance runners enrolled; 18 completed
intervention: Plyometric, sprint, and resistance work added to normal running
component_dose: Twice weekly for 10 weeks; secondary review reports 2-4 sets, 6-15 repetitions, 90-180 s rest
outcome_measure: Economy, sprint, MVC, aerobic and body-composition outcomes
instrument_protocol: Abstract and repository metadata; dose partly from secondary review
effect_estimate: Economy changes about 3.2-3.7% described as possibly beneficial; sprint and MVC effects .32 and .86; other changes trivial/small
uncertainty: Abstract only; small completer groups; magnitude-based descriptions
limitations: High youth directness but low sequence and separation directness; no safety or architecture inference

population: 25 trained runners; concurrent n=13 and control n=12
intervention: Explosive-strength training substituted for part of endurance training
component_dose: Eight weeks; 19% of endurance training replaced; secondary review reports one explosive session/week and 2-3 x 6-10 repetitions
outcome_measure: Maximal anaerobic running speed, 30-m speed, aerobic maximal speed, VO2max, economy
instrument_protocol: Abstract-level extraction with secondary-review dose context
effect_estimate: Anaerobic running speed +3.0 +/- 2.0% and 30-m speed +1.1 +/- 1.3%; aerobic outcomes unchanged
uncertainty: Abstract only; sequence and separation not reported
limitations: Evidence concerns component substitution, not a universal order or 9.5-day schedule

population: 12 competitive youth runners; concurrent n=6 and aerobic-only n=6
sex: Concurrent 3 male/3 female; control sex NOT_VERIFIED
intervention: Continuous and interval running plus machine resistance
component_dose: Ten weeks; running twice weekly; resistance frequency conflicts between abstract and youth-review table; review reports 3-4 x 10-12 at 70-75% 1RM
outcome_measure: 3-km time
instrument_protocol: Abstract-level trial report and secondary youth-review table
effect_estimate: No significant effects; 3-km change +6 s concurrent versus -17 s aerobic-only; post-test difference 38 s adverse to concurrent
uncertainty: Extremely small groups; unresolved dose; abstract only
limitations: Possible interference signal only; cannot prove sequence, safety, or calendar rules

population: 17 national/international male junior middle-distance runners
intervention: Depth jumps before submaximal running and time to exhaustion
comparator: Depth jumps versus body-weight quarter squats in randomized crossover
component_dose: Warm-up; economy run; six depth jumps; 10-min passive recovery; second economy run; time to exhaustion at vVO2max
outcome_measure: Running economy and time to exhaustion
instrument_protocol: Randomized crossover acute protocol; abstract-level extraction
time_points: Pre-activation run; 10 min after activation; time to exhaustion
effect_estimate: Economy improved 3.7% after depth jumps; time to exhaustion and other physiological measures trivial
uncertainty: Abstract only; acute potentiation cannot establish chronic scheduling
limitations: Narrow acute sequence evidence only; no adjacent-day, safety, or 9.5-day inference

population: 518 youth across 15 studies; 268 male and 250 female
event: Swimming, running, rowing and other youth endurance contexts
intervention: Concurrent strength plus endurance training
component_dose: Heterogeneous protocols and events; median event distance 275 m
outcome_measure: Cardiorespiratory fitness, economy, athletic performance, leg power
missingness: Order, separation, maximal-strength and hypertrophy evidence unavailable
effect_estimate: Concurrent versus endurance-only: cardiorespiratory SMD .04 and economy .16 not significant; performance SMD .41; adolescent subgroup .52 but no age-group interaction
uncertainty: Sports/events pooled; moderate heterogeneity; low sequence directness
limitations: Concurrent training not universally harmful, but no exact sequence, separation, safety, or Formation rule

population: 28 well-trained male collegiate distance runners
intervention: Complex training, heavy resistance, or high-repetition control alongside endurance
comparator: Complex versus heavy resistance versus high-repetition control
component_dose: Eight weeks; nine sessions/week; six endurance and three strength; CT 3 x 5 at 80-85% 1RM then 3 x 6 matched jumps; 4-min recovery
component_order: Morning road running then afternoon strength on M/W/F; within strength heavy resistance before plyometric
outcome_measure: 1RM, CMJ, drop jump, RSI, economy, 50 m, 5 km, VO2max
instrument_protocol: Full-text running, strength, jump and performance testing
effect_estimate: CT and heavy resistance improved multiple outcomes; CT additionally improved drop jump, RSI and economy at 16 km/h; VO2max unchanged
uncertainty: Small groups; matched allocation; young-adult male sample; no separation comparator
limitations: Supports narrow component pairing; cannot establish ideal shared-day gap, safety, or 9.5-day superiority

population: 36 highly competitive middle- and long-distance runners
intervention: Plyometric plus usual endurance training
component_dose: Six weeks; drop jumps at 20/40 cm, CMJ and related explosive work
outcome_measure: 2.4-km time, 20-m time, CMJ, 20/40-cm drop jump
effect_estimate: Intervention changes: 2.4 km -3.9%, 20 m -2.3%, CMJ +8.9%, drop jumps +12.7% and +16.7%; control did not improve
uncertainty: Abstract only; no scheduling comparison
limitations: Supports component inclusion only, not calendar position, safety, or 9.5-day mechanics

population: 30 moderately trained distance runners inexperienced in strength training
intervention: Concurrent strength and endurance training
component_dose: Ten weeks; strength and plyometric detail NOT_VERIFIED
outcome_measure: 2-km time, time to exhaustion, running biomechanics, body fat
missingness: Strength dose, sequence and separation unavailable in abstract
effect_estimate: 2-km time mean difference -11.3 s; time to exhaustion +59.1 s; body fat -1.05 kg; most biomechanics unchanged
uncertainty: Adult recreational runners; abstract only; low scheduling directness
limitations: No youth, ordering, safety, or 9.5-day inference

population: 23 moderately trained male runners; intervention n=12 and control n=11
intervention: Speed-endurance running followed by heavy resistance while running volume reduced
component_dose: Eight weeks; twice weekly 30-s speed-endurance repetitions with 3-min recovery then resistance at 80-90% 1RM; volume reduced 42%
component_order: Speed endurance before resistance in same session
outcome_measure: 10 km, 1500 m, Yo-Yo, economy, strength, VO2max, NHE1, adherence
instrument_protocol: Full-text running, physiological and muscle testing
adverse_events: Consent mentioned risks/discomfort; intervention adverse events NOT_REPORTED
effect_estimate: 10 km 44:11 to 42:30 by week 4; 1500 m 5:27 to 5:10 by week 8; economy and strength improved; VO2max unchanged
uncertainty: Adult male sample; no reverse-order comparator; concurrent work partly substituted for running
limitations: Narrow protocol example, not universal sequence, dose, safety, or Formation evidence

population: 20 well-trained runners across five small groups
intervention: Five uphill interval programs
component_dose: Six weeks; exact protocols NOT_VERIFIED from abstract
outcome_measure: 5-km performance, economy, neuromuscular and aerobic measures
missingness: Exact protocols and group sizes beyond approximately four per condition NOT_VERIFIED
effect_estimate: Across programs 5-km improvement about 2.0% +/- 0.6%; highest intensity most favorable for economy and neuromuscular measures; no clearly superior program
uncertainty: Very small cells and model-based comparisons
limitations: Supports hill component context only; no placement, safety, or 9.5-day inference

population: 14 recreational female runners; run HIIT n=7 and cycle HIIT n=7
intervention: Run or cycle HIIT while habitual training continued
component_dose: Twice weekly for four weeks; 6 x 2 min at 95% HRmax with 2-min active recovery then 4 x 1 min all-out with 90-s recovery
outcome_measure: Treadmill VO2max, 10-km time, cycle pace, RPE, soreness, CK
time_points: Pre/post four weeks; soreness and CK at 24 h
missingness: Habitual training placement not standardized or reported
effect_estimate: Run-HIIT treadmill VO2max 42.1 to 45.2; 10-km time unchanged; cycling RPE and soreness increased
uncertainty: n=14; older all-female sample; tiny groups
limitations: Cycling cannot be assumed interchangeable, lower-fatigue, or a recovery method

population: Seven older randomized trials; sample sizes 11-60; heterogeneous adults
intervention: Running-only, cycling-only, or combined running/cycling programs
component_dose: At least four weeks; heterogeneous protocols
outcome_measure: Treadmill VO2max, cycle VO2max, running performance
effect_estimate: Treadmill VO2max g=-.32, cycle VO2max g=-.34, running performance g=.02; all null with wide intervals
uncertainty: Only seven older trials; substantial risk-of-bias concerns and heterogeneity
limitations: Null pooled effects do not establish modality equivalence, recovery advantage, safety, or placement

population: 894 runners across 38 studies; 651 male and 243 female
intervention: High-load, submaximal, plyometric, isometric, and combined strength methods alongside running
component_dose: 6-40 weeks; 1-4 strength sessions/week
outcome_measure: Running performance, VO2max, vVO2max, maximal metabolic steady state, sprint
effect_estimate: Performance favored high-load ES -.469 and combined methods -1.035; plyometric-only not significant; aerobic and sprint outcomes not significant
uncertainty: Publication bias for combined-method performance; certainty very low to moderate
limitations: Moderate component directness but low adolescent and scheduling directness; no safety or Formation rule

population: 1090 adults across 43 concurrent-training studies
intervention: Concurrent aerobic and resistance training
component_dose: At least four weeks; running in 16 studies and cycling in 24
outcome_measure: Maximal strength, hypertrophy, explosive strength
effect_estimate: No clear maximal-strength or hypertrophy penalty; explosive strength SMD -.28; same-session subgroup -.31 and separated-at-least-3-h subgroup not significant
uncertainty: Adult and heterogeneous; subgroup comparison across studies is not a validated threshold
limitations: Outcome-specific caution only; cannot create universal youth separation or sequence rules

population: Healthy adults across 10 studies
intervention: Resistance then endurance versus endurance then resistance
component_dose: At least five weeks; heterogeneous dose
outcome_measure: Dynamic and static strength, hypertrophy, aerobic capacity, power
effect_estimate: Resistance then endurance favored dynamic strength +6.91%; no sequence effect for hypertrophy, static strength, or aerobic capacity
uncertainty: I2 66% for dynamic strength; not runner- or youth-specific
limitations: Does not justify universal strength-first ordering; priority and outcome matter

population: 29 healthy moderately active men; two concurrent groups n=10 each and resistance-only n=9
intervention: HIIT then resistance or resistance then HIIT
component_dose: Nine weeks; three days/week
outcome_measure: Strength, lean mass, VO2peak, peak aerobic power, lactate threshold, CMJ displacement/force/power
adverse_events: One withdrawal due to injury outside study; no intervention adverse event reported
effect_estimate: All groups strength +24-28% and lean mass +3-4%; concurrent groups improved aerobic outcomes; resistance-to-HIIT attenuated selected CMJ indices versus resistance-only
uncertainty: Healthy adult men; cycling not running; outcome-specific sequence effects
limitations: Conflicts with a simple strength-first rule and cannot set youth or 9.5-day mechanics

population: 58 adult amateur rugby players
intervention: Resistance followed by aerobic work with different recovery durations
comparator: Concurrent 0 h, 6 h, or 24 h; strength-only; control
component_dose: Two sessions of each quality per week for seven weeks
outcome_measure: Strength gains and VO2peak
missingness: Complete estimates and confidence intervals not available in abstract
effect_estimate: Strength gains lower at 0 h than 6 h, 24 h or strength-only; VO2peak improved in all concurrent groups
uncertainty: Magnitude-based inference; adult team sport; abstract only
limitations: One indirect study cannot define universal 6-h or 24-h youth thresholds

population: 18 healthy inactive adults after two weeks unilateral knee immobilization
intervention: Immediate endurance-to-resistance or resistance-to-endurance training
component_dose: Six weeks; three sessions/week; 22.5 min cycling at 65-75% VO2peak plus 3 x 10 leg exercises at 65-80% 1RM
outcome_measure: Gene expression, mitochondrial complex II, strength, muscle mass, VO2peak
time_horizon: Six-week adaptation and acute molecular response
adverse_events: Immobilization pain/edema monitored; training adverse events NOT_REPORTED
effect_estimate: Order affected mitochondrial complex II but not strength, muscle mass or VO2peak; acute gene-expression interactions null
uncertainty: Rehabilitation and detraining context; n=18; strongly indirect
limitations: Null rehab findings should not override trained-runner evidence or create universal order rules

population: Adults across 21 concurrent-training studies
intervention: Concurrent endurance and resistance training
component_dose: Heterogeneous modality, frequency and duration
component_order: Order and separation not recoverable from abstract
outcome_measure: Interference outcomes and moderators
effect_estimate: Interference signal reported particularly for power, varying with endurance mode and dose
uncertainty: Older synthesis overlapping later reviews; abstract only
limitations: Historical context only; no youth-runner calendar rule or universal fatigue number

population: 750 adults across 27 studies; 523 male and 227 female
intervention: Concurrent endurance and identical resistance training
component_dose: Heterogeneous protocols
outcome_measure: Lower-body one-repetition maximum
missingness: Two studies mixed separation and one did not report
effect_estimate: No significant penalty untrained or moderately trained; trained ES -.35; trained same-session ES -.66 and different-session null
uncertainty: Between-study subgroup result; adult and strength-specific; bins are not validated thresholds
limitations: Cannot determine endurance-first versus strength-first, adolescent separation, safety, or 9.5-day schedule

population: 15 well-trained adult cyclists; block n=8 and traditional n=7
intervention: Block periodization of high-intensity aerobic training
comparator: Traditional organization with two weekly HIT sessions
component_dose: Twelve weeks; block group performed five HIT sessions every fourth week followed by three weeks of one HIT session; total HIT and LIT volumes matched
component_order: Concentrated block versus evenly distributed HIT
outcome_measure: VO2max, power at 2 mmol/L lactate, hemoglobin mass, 40-min all-out power
effect_estimate: VO2max +8.8 +/- 5.9% block versus +3.7 +/- 2.9% traditional; moderate effects reported across selected indices
uncertainty: n=15; cycling; abstract only; several outcomes summarized by effect-size range
limitations: Small adult cycling study cannot establish running-frame length, 9.5-day superiority, or safety

population: 63 trained adult cyclists; increasing n=23, decreasing n=20, mixed n=20
intervention: Increasing, decreasing, or mixed order of three interval prescriptions
component_dose: Twelve weeks; 24 interval sessions plus high-volume low-intensity training; 4 x 16 min, 4 x 8 min, and 4 x 4 min mesocycles
component_order: Increasing versus reverse versus mixed mesocycle order
outcome_measure: 40-min all-out mean power, peak power, VO2peak, power at 4 mmol/L lactate
instrument_protocol: Randomized controlled cycling trial; abstract-level extraction
effect_estimate: All groups improved selected outcomes 5-10%; no between-group adaptation difference; response-category likelihood similar
uncertainty: Adult cycling and abstract-only evidence; interval order is not calendar-frame duration
individual_response: Similar likelihood of large, moderate, or nonresponse across groups
limitations: Counters a universal sequence effect but cannot prescribe a 9.5-day running architecture

population: One world-class adult female cross-country skier
intervention: Block-periodized versus traditionally periodized HIT across two historical seasons
comparator: Within-athlete comparison of two successful seasons
component_dose: Seven HIT blocks lasting 7-11 days with 8-13 HIT sessions each; block weeks averaged 16.2 +/- 1.3 h and 10.8 +/- 0.4 sessions
component_order: HIT blocks distributed across preparation phases; no blocks in competition phase
outcome_measure: Training volume, intensity distribution, TRIMP, competition rank, illness days
instrument_protocol: Training diaries, athlete/coach interviews, historical season comparison
missingness: Single-athlete historical records; no counterfactual season control
adverse_events: Illness days recorded; no intervention adverse-event inference
effect_estimate: Both models accompanied successful seasons; block season used twice as many HIT sessions and lower total volume; no causal effect estimate
uncertainty: n=1; retrospective case; skiing; seasons differ in more than block duration
limitations: Verifies 7-11-day block practice, not a recurring 9.5-day frame, causal benefit, youth transfer, or safety

population: World-class adult 800-m and 1500-m runners represented in scientific and best-practice literature
sex: Both sexes; literature biased toward men
intervention: Event-specific integration of training principles and practice
component_dose: Reported preparation volumes about 50-120 km/week for 800 m versus 120-170 km/week for 1500 m; training distributions about 60/40 versus 90/10 below versus at/above anaerobic threshold
component_order: Event-specific component emphasis; no exact microcycle comparison
outcome_measure: Aerobic/anaerobic distribution, volume, intervals, strength, power, plyometrics
instrument_protocol: Narrative review and position statement using scientific and best-practice literature
time_points: Annual phases and typical training organization
effect_estimate: 800- and 1500-m athletes show materially different volumes, distributions, and component emphasis
uncertainty: Narrative evidence; male and long-distance-thinking bias; no adolescent or cycle-length comparison
limitations: Supports event stratification only; not direct adolescent 9.5-day efficacy, safety, or automatic clearance evidence

population: Eight highly trained 1500-m runners
intervention: Two seven-day taper strategies in crossover
comparator: Race-pace taper versus lower-volume higher-intensity taper
component_dose: Race-pace: continuous volume -30% and final intervals at 1500-m pace; high-intensity: volume -60% and final intervals at 110% race pace
component_order: Two taper conditions separated by at least three weeks regular training
outcome_measure: 1500-m indoor time trial and 300-m segment pacing
time_points: Time trial one day before and one day after each taper
effect_estimate: Time improved 5.2 +/- 3.7 s after high-intensity taper and 3.2 +/- 3.8 s after race-pace taper; early 300-m segments faster after both
uncertainty: n=8; abstract only; two taper packages differ in volume and final intensity
individual_response: Within-athlete crossover with at least three-week washout
limitations: Supports taper-component direction in a tiny sample, not a 9.5-day taper rule, whole-frame benefit, or safety

population: 30 trained adult cyclists; 26 male and 4 female; block n=14 and traditional n=16
intervention: Load-matched block versus traditional periodization
comparator: Weekly concentration versus cyclic progressive distribution of HIT, MIT, and LIT
component_dose: Twelve weeks with matched training load; detailed session distribution varies by group
component_order: Block concentration versus weekly cyclic progression
outcome_measure: 5- and 40-min time-trial power, Wmax, W4mmol, VO2peak, red-cell volume, capillarization, enzymes, gross efficiency
time_points: Pre, intermediate, and post twelve weeks
adverse_events: Participants informed of risks/discomfort; intervention adverse events NOT_REPORTED
effect_estimate: Both groups improved time-trial power 8-9%, Wmax and W4mmol; no performance-group difference; hematological/capillary adaptations differed
uncertainty: Adult cycling; many outcomes; no running or nonweekly-frame comparison
limitations: Counters universal block superiority and cannot establish 9.5-day running mechanics, efficacy, or safety

population: 12 highly trained adult male swimmers
intervention: Front crawl under maximal inspiration, intermediate lung volume, and maximal expiration
component_dose: 15 m at target velocity 1.20 m/s
outcome_measure: Vertical center-of-mass position, trunk inclination, projected frontal area
instrument_protocol: Underwater motion capture and reconstructed digital-human model; abstract-level extraction
effect_estimate: Projected frontal area decreased with larger lung volume
uncertainty: Wrong identifier for the intended running-recovery citation; no recovery time course or running exposure
limitations: Canonical screening decision is EXCLUDE; this PMID must contribute no RQ-B recovery, 72-h, or prescription claim

population: 11 collegiate female distance runners; run n=6 and cycling-substitution n=5
intervention: Running only versus 50% cycling substitution on alternate days
component_dose: Daily training at 75-80% maximal HR; 40-50 miles/week seasonal volume; cycling represented 50% of volume in cross-training group
outcome_measure: 3000-m time and estimated VO2max
effect_estimate: 3000 m slowed 1.4% run-only versus 3.4% cross-training; estimated VO2max showed no important change
uncertainty: n=11; off-season; abstract only; estimated rather than directly measured VO2max
limitations: Cycling may preserve selected aerobic status but is not performance-equivalent, a recovery accelerator, or a Formation slot rule

population: 15 highly trained distance runners; plyometric n=7 and control n=8
intervention: Plyometric training added to normal running
component_dose: Three 30-min plyometric sessions/week for nine weeks
component_order: Placement relative to normal running NOT_REPORTED
outcome_measure: Economy at 14, 16, and 18 km/h, VO2max, jump power, strength-quality timing
effect_estimate: Economy improved 4.1% at 18 km/h versus control but not 14/16 km/h; no VO2max or cardiorespiratory difference
uncertainty: n=15; abstract only; scheduling and sex not reported
limitations: Supports context-specific plyometric adaptation only; no microcycle placement, safety, or 9.5-day inference

population: Long-distance runners across 22 studies
intervention: Heavy resistance or plyometric training as adjuncts to running
comparator: Heavy resistance versus plyometric method bodies; running controls within included studies
component_dose: At least four weeks; heavy resistance at least 70% 1RM for eligibility; method and duration subgroups
component_order: Order and running-session placement not analyzed
outcome_measure: Running economy and time-trial performance
instrument_protocol: Systematic review/meta-analysis with PubMed, Web of Science, and SPORTDiscus search
time_horizon: Four to fourteen weeks across subgroups
missingness: Study-level schedule fields and adverse events not synthesized
effect_estimate: Economy: heavy resistance g=-.32 [-.55,-.10] versus plyometric g=-.13 [-.47,.21]; selected duration/load subgroups differed
uncertainty: Review populations and protocols heterogeneous; source-reported heavy-resistance time-trial CI is internally inconsistent with its point estimate
limitations: Compares component methods only; cannot create a universal sequence, Formation interval, safety claim, or 9.5-day validation

population: 20 male high-school middle-distance runners; HIIT n=10 and MIIT n=10
intervention: High- versus medium-intensity interval training plus identical weight training
comparator: HIIT at 90-95% HRR versus MIIT at 60-70% HRR
component_dose: Ten weeks; IT M/W/F for 60 min including 20-min warm-up, 35-min intervals and 5-min cool-down; weights Tue/Sat for 60 min at 60-70% 1RM
component_order: Intervals Monday, Wednesday, Friday; weight training Tuesday and Saturday
outcome_measure: 800-m time, CK, LDH, SOD, MDA and other serum markers
instrument_protocol: Randomized full-text intervention; heart-rate reserve prescription and blood assays
missingness: Exact interval work/rest repetitions require table interpretation and are not normalized here
effect_estimate: Both groups improved 800-m records; improvement stronger in HIIT; CK decreased only in HIIT and SOD increased in HIIT; MDA unchanged
uncertainty: n=20; male only; maturity unreported; both groups also performed weights; biomarker change is not recovery or safety
limitations: Supports component outcomes only; no recovery clearance, adjacent-day safety, or whole-Formation inference

population: 28 well-trained adult male runners; strength n=14 and running-only n=14
event: Prolonged heavy-domain running and fatigued high-intensity performance
intervention: Supplementary maximal strength and plyometric training plus habitual running
component_dose: Twice weekly for ten weeks; exact strength and plyometric exercise dose NOT_VERIFIED from abstract
outcome_measure: Economy across 90-min heavy-intensity run and time to exhaustion at 95% pretest VO2max
time_points: Economy every 15 min during 90-min run; immediate time-to-exhaustion test
effect_estimate: Economy at 90 min changed -2.1% strength versus +0.6% control; time to exhaustion +35% versus -8%
uncertainty: Adult male sample; abstract only; no validated neural-fatigue measure; scheduling absent
limitations: Supports selected economy-durability outcomes, not neural-fatigue resistance, recovery, placement, safety, or 9.5-day architecture

population: 40 moderately trained middle-distance runners; 24 male and 16 female; four groups n=10 each
intervention: Shallow 2.5%, intermediate 5.1%, or steep 7.6% uphill training
comparator: Three gradient groups versus normal-training control
component_dose: Two uphill sessions/week for eight weeks at 85-100% HRmax, replacing strength training; normal running about 21.748 miles/week; gradient-specific bouts and rests
component_order: Hill sessions added to normal running; exact weekly placement NOT_REPORTED
outcome_measure: 800-m time trial, flying 30-m maximal velocity, one-minute burpees
instrument_protocol: Randomized full-text trial with video timing and heart-rate monitoring
time_points: Pre and 48 h after intervention; tests over two days
missingness: At least 85% attendance required; all analyzed n=40
adverse_events: Injury history screened; intervention adverse events NOT_REPORTED
effect_estimate: Steep group outperformed control for Vmax, 800 m and burpees; intermediate group also improved 800 m versus control
uncertainty: Small four-arm study; age spans adolescent/young adult; strength training was substituted; no maturity measure
limitations: Supports uphill component adaptation in this sample only; no neural-fatigue, Formation-slot, safety, or 9.5-day inference

population: 30 well-trained adults; 10 male and 20 female
event: 3.2-km running with running or swimming cross-training
intervention: Added running, added swimming cross-training, or continued baseline running
comparator: Running addition versus swimming cross-training versus control
component_dose: Eight weeks; training enhanced 10% per week in running or swimming groups
outcome_measure: 3.2-km time, running and arm-crank VO2peak, submaximal VO2, velocity at VO2peak, lactate-threshold velocity
effect_estimate: 3.2-km time changed -26.4 s running, -13.2 s swimming, -5.4 s control; running lactate velocity improved only with added running; arm-crank outcomes improved with swimming
uncertainty: Older abstract-only study; swimming is mechanically dissimilar; group sizes and schedule details not reported
limitations: Partial aerobic transfer is not mode equivalence, recovery acceleration, safe substitution, or a Formation placement rule
